description: Attack preconditions covering the full nginx attack surface: version disclosure, path traversal, SSRF via proxy_pass, weak TLS, missing security headers, unrestricted HTTP methods, and configuration exposure.

N-01.label: nginx_reachable
N-01.description: nginx is listening on a network port and responding to HTTP requests from the attacker's position.
N-01.evidence_hint: Check ss/netstat for nginx listen sockets on :80/:443/:8080; confirm with HTTP HEAD response containing HTTP/ status line.

N-02.label: version_disclosed
N-02.description: nginx version is exposed in the Server response header or in HTTP error page bodies, enabling targeted exploit selection.
N-02.evidence_hint: Send HTTP request and inspect Server: header and error response body for 'nginx/X.Y.Z' pattern.

N-03.label: directory_listing
N-03.description: autoindex is enabled for one or more location blocks, causing nginx to render directory listings that may expose sensitive file paths.
N-03.evidence_hint: Fetch a directory URL and check response body for 'Index of' or 'directory listing'; inspect nginx config for 'autoindex on'.

N-04.label: config_readable
N-04.description: /etc/nginx/nginx.conf or full configuration dump (nginx -T) is readable via SSH, exposing backend topology, auth bypass opportunities, and misconfigurations.
N-04.evidence_hint: Run 'nginx -T' or read /etc/nginx/nginx.conf; non-empty output with directive syntax confirms readability.

N-05.label: alias_path_traversal
N-05.description: An alias directive ends with / but its enclosing location block path does not end with /, creating the classic nginx alias path traversal that allows reading files outside the intended directory.
N-05.evidence_hint: Inspect nginx config for pattern: location /X { alias /path/; } where /X has no trailing slash. Test with request to /X../etc/passwd.

N-06.label: proxy_pass_internal
N-06.description: A proxy_pass directive forwards requests to an internal network address (127.x, 10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x), creating a Server-Side Request Forgery vector.
N-06.evidence_hint: Inspect nginx config for proxy_pass directives targeting RFC-1918 or loopback addresses.

N-07.label: server_tokens_on
N-07.description: server_tokens is not set to off, causing nginx to include the full version string in the Server header and error pages.
N-07.evidence_hint: Check nginx config for absence of 'server_tokens off'; confirm version visible in Server header on any response.

N-08.label: no_security_headers
N-08.description: Responses are missing two or more of: X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security. Absence enables clickjacking, MIME-sniffing, and protocol downgrade attacks.
N-08.evidence_hint: Inspect response headers from HTTP HEAD request for presence of X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security.

N-09.label: http_methods_unrestricted
N-09.description: No limit_except directive restricts HTTP methods, allowing PUT, DELETE, and other dangerous verbs to reach upstream application servers or static locations.
N-09.evidence_hint: Check nginx config for absence of limit_except blocks; probe with HTTP PUT to confirm method is forwarded rather than rejected at the nginx layer.

N-10.label: weak_tls
N-10.description: ssl_protocols directive includes SSLv2, SSLv3, or TLSv1 (without .1/.2 suffix), enabling downgrade attacks and use of broken cipher suites.
N-10.evidence_hint: Inspect nginx config for ssl_protocols directive; check for SSLv2, SSLv3, TLSv1 entries.

N-11.label: open_redirect
N-11.description: A return or rewrite directive incorporates a user-controlled request variable (e.g., $arg_*, $http_*) in the redirect target, enabling open redirect exploitation.
N-11.evidence_hint: Scan nginx config for return or rewrite directives that embed $arg_ or $http_ variables in redirect URIs.

N-12.label: client_max_body_size_unlimited
N-12.description: client_max_body_size is either not set (nginx default 1m applies but is often overridden to 0) or explicitly set to 0 (unlimited), enabling Denial-of-Service via arbitrarily large request bodies.
N-12.evidence_hint: Search nginx config for 'client_max_body_size'; value of 0 or absence in a context where upstream accepts large bodies indicates risk.

nginx_info_disclosure_v1.label: Nginx Version Fingerprinting Chain
nginx_info_disclosure_v1.description: nginx is reachable, version is disclosed via Server header or error pages, and server_tokens is not suppressed — attacker can precisely fingerprint the nginx version and select targeted exploits.

nginx_path_traversal_v1.label: Nginx Alias Path Traversal
nginx_path_traversal_v1.description: nginx is reachable, config is readable confirming the alias misconfiguration, and the alias directive has the trailing-slash mismatch that allows path traversal outside the intended directory.

nginx_ssrf_v1.label: SSRF via proxy_pass to Internal Service
nginx_ssrf_v1.description: nginx is reachable, config is readable exposing the proxy_pass target, and proxy_pass forwards to an internal RFC-1918 or loopback address — attacker can pivot to internal services.

nginx_file_upload_dos_v1.label: Unrestricted Upload Denial-of-Service
nginx_file_upload_dos_v1.description: nginx is reachable, HTTP methods are unrestricted at the nginx layer (no limit_except), and client_max_body_size is unlimited — attacker can exhaust disk or memory via unbounded POST/PUT.
